title: Flail Chest
oneLiner: Multiple rib fractures (≥2 fractures in ≥2 ribs) causing paradoxical chest wall movement; often with pulmonary contusion, risk of respiratory failure.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax** — tracheal deviation, distended neck veins, hyperresonance, hypotension. Single finding: **tracheal deviation away** + absent breath sounds.
- **Massive haemothorax** — dull percussion, shock, absent breath sounds. Single finding: **failure of chest drain output to clear** or >1500 mL initial drainage.
- **Cardiac tamponade** (rare with rib fractures, but consider if penetrating trauma) — Beck’s triad (muffled heart sounds, hypotension, distended neck veins), pulsus paradoxus.
- **Airway obstruction** from haemoptysis or secretions in patient unable to clear due to pain.

> **Priming rule:** Before touching the flail segment, **put your hand on the trachea** and **percuss both hemithoraces**. If either is shifted or dull/hyperresonant, treat the killer first.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Severe chest wall pain (pleuritic, worse with breathing/coughing)
  - Dyspnoea (often out of proportion to visible injury)
  - Cough with haemoptysis (suggests underlying contusion)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Paradoxical movement** — segment moves *in* on inspiration, *out* on expiration (may be subtle early)
  - Chest wall bruising, crepitus, tenderness
  - Tachypnoea, accessory muscle use, intercostal recession
  - Hypoxia (SpO₂ <92% on air) common
  - Signs of associated injuries: pneumothorax, haemothorax, pulmonary contusion, cardiac/spleen/liver trauma
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Simple rib fracture(s)** – no paradoxical movement, segment moves normally; pain but less respiratory compromise
  - **Pulmonary contusion without flail** – hypoxia, opacification on CXR/CT, but no paradoxical movement
  - **Pneumothorax** – hyperresonance, absent breath sounds, no segmental paradoxical movement
  - **Haemothorax** – dullness, shock, no paradoxical movement
  - **Costochondral separation** – focal tenderness at costochondral junction, no paradoxical movement
- **Splitter:** **Bilateral flail segments** (e.g. sternal fracture) – extremely high risk of respiratory failure; any paradoxical movement mandates consideration of flail chest even if CXR appears normal
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Assess airway, breathing, circulation** (ABC)
  - SpO₂, respiratory rate, blood pressure
  - **Trial of bag-valve-mask ventilation** if in respiratory failure? Only if intubating.
  - ECG – rule out myocardial contusion (arrhythmias, ST changes) if high-energy mechanism
- **Bloods:**
  - ABG: PaO₂, PaCO₂, pH (hypoxia, hypercapnia early)
  - FBC, clotting screen, crossmatch (if haemothorax or major trauma)
  - Lactate (shock marker), U&E (baseline)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **Chest X-ray (erect if possible)** – look for rib fractures (≥2 ribs fractured in ≥2 places), pneumothorax, haemothorax, pulmonary contusion. *Note: up to 50% of rib fractures missed on CXR.*
  - **Second-line:** **CT chest (with contrast)** – gold standard: defines exact fracture pattern, assesses contusion, rules out great vessel injury. Perform in all patients with high-energy trauma or clinical suspicion of flail.
- **Special tests:** None routine.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical (paradoxical movement) + imaging (≥2 ribs, ≥2 fractures) = flail chest. Severe pulmonary contusion often coexists.
- **Never-miss test:** **CT chest** – missing a flail segment or contusion on CXR leads to underestimation of respiratory risk. *Also: ECG in any sternal fracture or high-speed deceleration.*
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **High-flow oxygen** via non-rebreather mask (target SpO₂ >92%)
  - **Analgesia** – paracetamol 1g IV/PO + consider morphine 2.5–5 mg IV titrated (but **[check local policy]** for exact dosing)
  - **Consider NIV** (CPAP or BiPAP) – splints chest wall, improves oxygenation. Start in ED if safe airway and no contraindications (e.g. untreated pneumothorax, impaired consciousness)
  - **Chest drain insertion** if pneumothorax/haemothorax present – use blunt dissection technique
  - **Consider early intubation** if: respiratory rate >30, SpO₂ <90% despite high-flow O₂, PaCO₂ >7.0 kPa, GCS <15, or shock
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Admit to HDU/ITU** (not general ward) if bilateral flail, severe contusion, age >65, or any comorbidity (COPD, IHD)
  - **Respiratory physiotherapy** – early mobilisation, incentive spirometry, manual techniques
  - **Analgesia** – multimodal: paracetamol, NSAIDs (if no renal/bleeding risk), ± epidural analgesia (if severe, discuss with pain team)
  - **NIV** as step-down from ITU or primary if not intubated
  - **Monitor for deterioration** – NEWS2, ABGs, SpO₂ trends
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Surgery** (rib fixation) – controversial; consider in severe flail with respiratory failure not responding to NIV, or with chest wall deformity. Not routine.
  - **Pain management** – early physiotherapy, oral analgesia as needed
  - **Follow-up** – clinic in 6 weeks; check for non-union, chronic pain
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann’s) at 1 mL/kg/h unless hypovolaemic; avoid overload in contusion
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if no bleeding risk; start 12h after injury if no active bleeding)
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM only if intubated or awaiting surgery; otherwise start oral intake as tolerated
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 every 15 minutes initially then hourly; continuous SpO₂ monitoring
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Paracetamol** – 1 g IV/PO QDS (max 4 g/day)
  - **Morphine** – 2.5–5 mg IV, titrate to pain; if PCA used, **[check local policy]**
  - **Ibuprofen** (NSAIDs) – 400 mg PO TDS (if no renal impairment, GI bleed risk, or severe contusion)
  - **Epidural analgesia** – bupivacaine + fentanyl via epidural catheter; only after discussion with anaesthetics/pain team
- **Key contraindications:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if AKI, eGFR <30, active bleeding, coagulopathy, or severe contusion (risk of bleeding)
  - Morphine: caution in hypotension, bradypnoea; always have naloxone 400 mcg IV available
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Paracetamol: reduced dose in severe hepatic impairment (no specific UK dose; check BNF)
  - Morphine: reduce dose in renal impairment (eGFR <30)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  **Over-sedation with opioids leading to respiratory depression** – always prescribe naloxone alongside PCA; monitor sedation score and respiratory rate. *Never give morphine IV push in a patient with low GCS or without pulse oximetry.*
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure (need for intubation)
  - Pneumonia (due to poor cough, retained secretions)
  - Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) – especially if contusion large
  - Haemothorax/pneumothorax recurrence
  - Cardiac contusion (arrhythmias, low ejection fraction)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chest wall deformity, non-union of fractures
  - Chronic pain (neuropathic or mechanical)
  - Impaired lung function (restrictive pattern)
  - Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in major trauma
sections.Prognosis:
- Mortality ~10–20% overall; higher with bilateral flail, age >65, severe contusion, or multi-trauma
- Majority recover lung function by 3–6 months if no ARDS; chronic pain common in ~30–50%
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Start NIV early** (CPAP 5–10 cmH₂O) – splints chest wall, reduces work of breathing, prevents intubation
- **Involve respiratory physiotherapy within 24 hours** – even before full radiology results
- **Order a CT chest** if CXR shows any rib fractures (even if no clear flail) – many flail segments are posterior and missed on X-ray
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t rely on CXR alone** – a normal CXR does not rule out flail chest or severe pulmonary contusion
- **Don’t give opioids without monitoring** – respiratory depression combined with contusion can tip patient into failure
- **Don’t discharge from ED** – all patients with possible flail chest require hospital admission (≥24h observation)
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home) *:
- **Return if:** worsening breathlessness, fever >38°C, new haemoptysis, severe pain unresponsive to oral analgesia
- **Follow-up:** Chest clinic review in 2 weeks for repeat CXR; if on anticoagulation, discuss VTE prophylaxis
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) or ITU outreach if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or rising)
  - SpO₂ <92% despite FiO₂ ≥0.6 (high-flow mask or NIV)
  - Respiratory rate >30 despite analgesia
  - GCS <15 (new)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or rising
  - Need for intubation (call anaesthetics immediately)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - PaO₂ <8 kPa on ABG → escalate
  - PaCO₂ >7.0 kPa → consider NIV or intubation
  - Chest drain output >1500 mL initially or >200 mL/h for >2 hours → call cardiothoracic surgical team

rawMarkdown:
## Flail Chest
**One-liner:** Multiple rib fractures (≥2 fractures in ≥2 ribs) causing paradoxical chest wall movement; often with pulmonary contusion, risk of respiratory failure.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension pneumothorax** — tracheal deviation, distended neck veins, hyperresonance, hypotension. Single finding: **tracheal deviation away** + absent breath sounds.
- **Massive haemothorax** — dull percussion, shock, absent breath sounds. Single finding: **failure of chest drain output to clear** or >1500 mL initial drainage.
- **Cardiac tamponade** (rare with rib fractures, but consider if penetrating trauma) — Beck’s triad (muffled heart sounds, hypotension, distended neck veins), pulsus paradoxus.
- **Airway obstruction** from haemoptysis or secretions in patient unable to clear due to pain.

> **Priming rule:** Before touching the flail segment, **put your hand on the trachea** and **percuss both hemithoraces**. If either is shifted or dull/hyperresonant, treat the killer first.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Severe chest wall pain (pleuritic, worse with breathing/coughing)
  - Dyspnoea (often out of proportion to visible injury)
  - Cough with haemoptysis (suggests underlying contusion)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Paradoxical movement** — segment moves *in* on inspiration, *out* on expiration (may be subtle early)
  - Chest wall bruising, crepitus, tenderness
  - Tachypnoea, accessory muscle use, intercostal recession
  - Hypoxia (SpO₂ <92% on air) common
  - Signs of associated injuries: pneumothorax, haemothorax, pulmonary contusion, cardiac/spleen/liver trauma

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Simple rib fracture(s)** – no paradoxical movement, segment moves normally; pain but less respiratory compromise
  - **Pulmonary contusion without flail** – hypoxia, opacification on CXR/CT, but no paradoxical movement
  - **Pneumothorax** – hyperresonance, absent breath sounds, no segmental paradoxical movement
  - **Haemothorax** – dullness, shock, no paradoxical movement
  - **Costochondral separation** – focal tenderness at costochondral junction, no paradoxical movement
- **Splitter:** **Bilateral flail segments** (e.g. sternal fracture) – extremely high risk of respiratory failure; any paradoxical movement mandates consideration of flail chest even if CXR appears normal

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **Assess airway, breathing, circulation** (ABC)
  - SpO₂, respiratory rate, blood pressure
  - **Trial of bag-valve-mask ventilation** if in respiratory failure? Only if intubating.
  - ECG – rule out myocardial contusion (arrhythmias, ST changes) if high-energy mechanism
- **Bloods:**
  - ABG: PaO₂, PaCO₂, pH (hypoxia, hypercapnia early)
  - FBC, clotting screen, crossmatch (if haemothorax or major trauma)
  - Lactate (shock marker), U&E (baseline)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **Chest X-ray (erect if possible)** – look for rib fractures (≥2 ribs fractured in ≥2 places), pneumothorax, haemothorax, pulmonary contusion. *Note: up to 50% of rib fractures missed on CXR.*
  - **Second-line:** **CT chest (with contrast)** – gold standard: defines exact fracture pattern, assesses contusion, rules out great vessel injury. Perform in all patients with high-energy trauma or clinical suspicion of flail.
- **Special tests:** None routine.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical (paradoxical movement) + imaging (≥2 ribs, ≥2 fractures) = flail chest. Severe pulmonary contusion often coexists.
- **Never-miss test:** **CT chest** – missing a flail segment or contusion on CXR leads to underestimation of respiratory risk. *Also: ECG in any sternal fracture or high-speed deceleration.*

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **High-flow oxygen** via non-rebreather mask (target SpO₂ >92%)
  - **Analgesia** – paracetamol 1g IV/PO + consider morphine 2.5–5 mg IV titrated (but **[check local policy]** for exact dosing)
  - **Consider NIV** (CPAP or BiPAP) – splints chest wall, improves oxygenation. Start in ED if safe airway and no contraindications (e.g. untreated pneumothorax, impaired consciousness)
  - **Chest drain insertion** if pneumothorax/haemothorax present – use blunt dissection technique
  - **Consider early intubation** if: respiratory rate >30, SpO₂ <90% despite high-flow O₂, PaCO₂ >7.0 kPa, GCS <15, or shock
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Admit to HDU/ITU** (not general ward) if bilateral flail, severe contusion, age >65, or any comorbidity (COPD, IHD)
  - **Respiratory physiotherapy** – early mobilisation, incentive spirometry, manual techniques
  - **Analgesia** – multimodal: paracetamol, NSAIDs (if no renal/bleeding risk), ± epidural analgesia (if severe, discuss with pain team)
  - **NIV** as step-down from ITU or primary if not intubated
  - **Monitor for deterioration** – NEWS2, ABGs, SpO₂ trends
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Surgery** (rib fixation) – controversial; consider in severe flail with respiratory failure not responding to NIV, or with chest wall deformity. Not routine.
  - **Pain management** – early physiotherapy, oral analgesia as needed
  - **Follow-up** – clinic in 6 weeks; check for non-union, chronic pain
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann’s) at 1 mL/kg/h unless hypovolaemic; avoid overload in contusion
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if no bleeding risk; start 12h after injury if no active bleeding)
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM only if intubated or awaiting surgery; otherwise start oral intake as tolerated
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 every 15 minutes initially then hourly; continuous SpO₂ monitoring

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Paracetamol** – 1 g IV/PO QDS (max 4 g/day)
  - **Morphine** – 2.5–5 mg IV, titrate to pain; if PCA used, **[check local policy]**
  - **Ibuprofen** (NSAIDs) – 400 mg PO TDS (if no renal impairment, GI bleed risk, or severe contusion)
  - **Epidural analgesia** – bupivacaine + fentanyl via epidural catheter; only after discussion with anaesthetics/pain team
- **Key contraindications:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if AKI, eGFR <30, active bleeding, coagulopathy, or severe contusion (risk of bleeding)
  - Morphine: caution in hypotension, bradypnoea; always have naloxone 400 mcg IV available
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Paracetamol: reduced dose in severe hepatic impairment (no specific UK dose; check BNF)
  - Morphine: reduce dose in renal impairment (eGFR <30)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  **Over-sedation with opioids leading to respiratory depression** – always prescribe naloxone alongside PCA; monitor sedation score and respiratory rate. *Never give morphine IV push in a patient with low GCS or without pulse oximetry.*

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure (need for intubation)
  - Pneumonia (due to poor cough, retained secretions)
  - Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) – especially if contusion large
  - Haemothorax/pneumothorax recurrence
  - Cardiac contusion (arrhythmias, low ejection fraction)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chest wall deformity, non-union of fractures
  - Chronic pain (neuropathic or mechanical)
  - Impaired lung function (restrictive pattern)
  - Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in major trauma

### Prognosis
- Mortality ~10–20% overall; higher with bilateral flail, age >65, severe contusion, or multi-trauma
- Majority recover lung function by 3–6 months if no ARDS; chronic pain common in ~30–50%

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Start NIV early** (CPAP 5–10 cmH₂O) – splints chest wall, reduces work of breathing, prevents intubation
- **Involve respiratory physiotherapy within 24 hours** – even before full radiology results
- **Order a CT chest** if CXR shows any rib fractures (even if no clear flail) – many flail segments are posterior and missed on X-ray

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t rely on CXR alone** – a normal CXR does not rule out flail chest or severe pulmonary contusion
- **Don’t give opioids without monitoring** – respiratory depression combined with contusion can tip patient into failure
- **Don’t discharge from ED** – all patients with possible flail chest require hospital admission (≥24h observation)

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)  *(rare, only if isolated simple rib fractures and no flail)*
- **Return if:** worsening breathlessness, fever >38°C, new haemoptysis, severe pain unresponsive to oral analgesia
- **Follow-up:** Chest clinic review in 2 weeks for repeat CXR; if on anticoagulation, discuss VTE prophylaxis

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) or ITU outreach if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or rising)
  - SpO₂ <92% despite FiO₂ ≥0.6 (high-flow mask or NIV)
  - Respiratory rate >30 despite analgesia
  - GCS <15 (new)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or rising
  - Need for intubation (call anaesthetics immediately)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - PaO₂ <8 kPa on ABG → escalate
  - PaCO₂ >7.0 kPa → consider NIV or intubation
  - Chest drain output >1500 mL initially or >200 mL/h for >2 hours → call cardiothoracic surgical team
